data.title: Riley Exploration Permian GAAP EPS of $4.02 beats by $1.40, revenue of $97.2M misses by $11.27M
data.description:
• Riley Exploration Permian press release (REPX): Q4 GAAP EPS of $4.02 beats by $1.40.
  • Revenue of $97.2M (-5.3% Y/Y) misses by $11.27M.
  • Generated $65 million of operating cash flow, $85 million of net income, $66 million of Adjusted EBITDAX(1), $1 million of Total Free Cash Flow(1) and $17 million of Upstream Free Cash Flow(1)
  • Incurred total accrual (activity-based) capital expenditures before acquisitions of $50 million ($28 million for upstream)
  • Sold all of our membership interests in Dovetail Midstream LLC, a wholly owned subsidiary of the Company that held certain midstream infrastructure projects in Eddy County, New Mexico for total cash consideration of $123 million, with the right to earn up to an additional $60 million in cash payments contingent upon achieving certain volumetric performance thresholds over a five-year period ("Midstream Sale")
  • Reduced debt outstanding by $120 million with a year-end debt-to-Adjusted EBITDAX(1) ratio of 1.0x(2)
  • Announced the authorization of a stock repurchase program of up to $100 million of the currently outstanding shares of the Company's common stock
  •

data.title: Franklin Resources AUM rises 1.8% in February
data.description:
Franklin Resources (BEN), known as Franklin Templeton, reported  assets under management of $1.74T at Feb. 28, 2026, up 1.8% from $1.71T at the end of January, the company said Wednesday.

The month's AUM reflected the impact of positive markets and long-term net inflows of ~$10B, including ~$1B of long-term net outflows at Western Asset Management. Excluding Western Asset Management, preliminary net inflows were ~$11B.

Equity AUM rose 1.8% to $721.8B during the month, while fixed income AUM of $443.9B increased 0.7% M/M. Alternative AUM rose 1.1% to $278.4B, and multi-asset AUM grew 3.0% to $210.7B.

data.title: Hyster-Yale outlines $85M–$100M annual savings by 2028 amid market recovery and cost actions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hyster-Yale, Inc. (HY) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Rajiv Prasad, President and CEO, highlighted that “bookings in the fourth quarter strengthened significantly, increasing 42% sequentially and 35% year-over-year, which may signal the early stages of a demand recovery following an extended period of customer caution.” He noted continued momentum into the first two months of 2026, with North America leading the improvement.
  • Prasad explained the company’s strategic priorities: “Our priorities remain the same: rigorous working capital management, tight operational discipline, accelerated technology and product development and continuous data-driven monitoring of leading indicators across customers and suppliers.” He emphasized transformation programs, including new modular and scalable platforms, operational efficiency, manufacturing flexibility, customer engagement, product innovation, market readiness, and global optimization.
  • Prasad detailed decisive actions to lower costs, referencing “Nuvera's strategic realignment executed in the second quarter of 2025, delivered $15 million of cost savings in 2025” and a “company-wide restructuring program launched in quarter 4 of 2025 target $40 million to $45 million of annualized savings beginning in 2026.” Additional manufacturing footprint optimization is expected to deliver $20 million to $30 million in benefit in 2027, with full annualized savings of $30 million to $40 million by 2028, totaling $85 million to $100 million in recurring annualized savings by 2028 compared to the beginning of 2025.
  • Prasad addressed tariffs, stating, “forecasted tariff costs are expected to remain broadly consistent with quarter 4 2025 levels throughout 2026,” and while mitigation measures are underway, “we do not expect to fully offset tariff impacts.”
  • Executive Chairman Alfred Rankin stated, “2025 was a challenging year for our industry,” but emphasized structural improvements: “They are structural improvements that position us to perform better across cycles.”

Outlook

  • Management expects “quarter 1 2026 to mark the trough of the current cycle,” followed by improving bookings, backlog rebuilding, and stronger shipment volumes in the second half of 2026. Prasad outlined, “we expect moderate full year operating profit, a small loss in the first half, followed by stronger revenue and profit improvement in the second half.” The company aims for capital expenditures between $55 million and $75 million for 2026, dependent on production improvements.
  • Management remains “cautiously optimistic,” anticipating volume recovery and margin improvements in the back half of 2026, supported by structural cost reductions and new product traction.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenues declined to $923 million, reflecting lower shipment volumes “as customers continue to delay purchases until they have a clear need for new trucks.”
  • Operating cash flow increased to $57 million, driven by “meaningful improvements in inventory efficiency.” Fourth quarter adjusted operating loss totaled $16 million, including $40 million in gross tariff costs. For the full year, revenue was $3.8 billion and adjusted operating profit was $16 million, with approximately $100 million in gross tariff costs.
  • Backlog ended at $1.28 billion, down sequentially, reflecting “shipments outpacing new orders, especially within EMEA.”

Q&A

  • Alfred Moore, ROTH MKM, asked about pent-up demand and fleet replacement. Rajiv Prasad responded, “they are transitioning from...conserving to really ensuring that they will have what they need for their operations,” noting programs to catalyze engagement and a shift to counterbalance trucks.
  • Moore also inquired about new product launches and automation. Prasad shared, “just this week, we are launching some new products...our modular and scalable kind of spreading of that platform into our electric counterbalance trucks,” adding that “the actual official launch of the automated IDA truck is in April.”
  • Edward Jackson, Northland Capital Markets, summarized mix trends and margin normalization. Prasad confirmed, “I think that's directionally very correct,” anticipating gross margins “between mid-teens to high teens, which is where our targets are.”
  • Jackson asked about CapEx. Prasad outlined investments in “product...technology solutions, including automation and lithium-ion...IT infrastructure” and optimizing manufacturing footprint.
  • Jackson sought an update on warehouse segment penetration. Prasad indicated “progress in that area, especially in North America,” attributing success to new product launches and advanced safety and automation solutions.
  • Kirk Ludtke, Imperial Capital, questioned order rates by end market and automation margin impact. Prasad stated recovery is “in...industrials and more on the heavy side,” and that automation and lithium-ion solutions offer “quite a bump in revenue” and higher margins, though current autonomous sales are “tiny.”
  • Ludtke asked about tariff mitigation flexibility. Prasad explained, “we have now the ability to do that,” with ongoing monthly reviews to optimize production sourcing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on demand recovery, margin normalization, and the strategic impact of automation and new product launches. Their questions reflected cautious optimism with continued attention on mix, margin, and capital allocation.
  • Management maintained a consistently confident and disciplined tone in prepared remarks, stating, “we are confident in our ability,” but acknowledged ongoing challenges, especially related to tariffs and market mix. During Q&A, management provided thorough responses but were measured, avoiding overpromising on timing and margin expansion.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts expressed a more constructive outlook, with a shift from defensive explanations to cautious optimism and strategic focus on recovery and transformation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a notable shift from the previous quarter’s caution to a more optimistic stance, driven by a significant uptick in bookings and early signs of demand recovery, particularly in North America.
  • Strategic focus has evolved from navigating immediate downturn challenges and cost controls to actively positioning for recovery, with expanded transformation initiatives and cost-saving programs targeting up to $100 million in annual savings by 2028.
  • Analysts’ questions in the current quarter centered on the sustainability of demand recovery, margin normalization, and new product impacts, in contrast to prior concerns about persistent market softness and backlog profitability.
  • Management’s tone is more confident, emphasizing readiness, efficiency, and first-mover positioning, compared to the more cautious, defensive posture in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs remain a significant external headwind, projected to continue at current levels through 2026. Management does not expect mitigation efforts to fully offset these impacts in the near term.
  • Ongoing market shift toward lower-priced, lighter-duty equipment is compressing margins, especially in EMEA and JAPIC, and competitive pricing from foreign manufacturers remains aggressive.
  • Volume recovery is expected to be gradual, with near-term margin pressure persisting until new products gain broader traction.
  • Legal and policy uncertainties around tariffs could affect future cost structures and potential recoveries, with recent Supreme Court decisions providing limited clarity.

Final Takeaway

Hyster-Yale management conveyed cautious optimism as the company enters 2026, citing strong sequential booking growth, disciplined cost and inventory management, and early signs of a recovery in North America. Structural transformation programs, including modular product introductions, manufacturing optimization, and targeted cost reductions, are expected to drive up to $100 million in annual savings by 2028. Despite persistent tariff and margin headwinds, the company is positioned to capitalize on improving demand and remains committed to generating strong operating cash flow, investing in innovation, and maintaining capital discipline to support long-term value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Equinor, BP sign framework deal to advance C$14B Bay du Nord development
data.description:
Newfoundland and Labrador’s provincial government signed a framework agreement with Equinor (EQNR) and BP (BP) to advance construction of the long-delayed C$14B Bay du Nord offshore oil project, CBC reported Tuesday.

The government said the agreement, which also allows the province to take an equity stake in the project of as much as 10%, will provide up to $6.4B in direct revenue to the province in the first phase of the project.

If the project proceeds, it will become the province's first new standalone offshore oil and gas development since Hebron, its first deepwater project and fourth producing offshore oilfield; a final investment decision is scheduled for next year, with first oil planned for 2031.

Equinor (EQNR) said Bay du Nord will require an investment of ~C$14B, create thousands of jobs, and deliver tens of billions in royalties and taxes over its life.

data.title: American Eagle Outfitters tops Q4 estimates, but tepid outlook, margin pressure fade gains
data.description:
American Eagle (AEO) shares were briefly jolted higher as Wall Street reacted positively to a top- and bottom-line beat but faded the knee-jerk gains on the retailer’s underwhelming outlook for the full year, tariff-related margin pressure, and 10% increase in inventory at the end of the year.

For the fourth quarter, an 8% increase in comparable store sales soundly beat 1.3% estimates, led by a 23% surge in traffic at its Aerie banner (versus +6% in 2024). This helped generate a 10% increase in sales to $1.76B, beating expectations by $20M, and contributed to an adjusted profit of $0.84 per share, up 30 cents from a year ago and 13 cents above estimates.

Gross margin, however, was compressed by 30 basis points to 37% due to a net tariff impact of 280 basis points, and as increased markdowns were largely offset by leverage on positive sales combined with lower costs, favorable currency exchange rates, and operational efficiencies.

For the current quarter, American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) expects comparable sales to increase by high single digits, gross margin to be up from a year earlier, and operating income of $20M to $25M versus the $15M estimate from two analysts.

For FY26, comparable sales are seen increasing mid-single digits and operating income of $390M to $410M. Additionally, the company expects to spend between $250M and $260M on capital expenditures, almost half of what was anticipated. 

“We enter 2026 from a position of strength with the goal of building on this year’s successes. The first quarter is off to a positive start, and we remain focused on investing in our brands and driving additional corporate savings and efficiency across the business. I’m confident that our strategic actions will lead to long-term profitable growth and shareholder value creation,” he concluded.
